title: When The Ladies Come To The Shearing Shed
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The ladies are coming,ן¿½ the super says
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the shearers sweltering there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½the ladiesן¿½ means in the shearing shed:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Donן¿½t cut ן¿½em too bad. Donן¿½t swear.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The ghost of a pause in the shedן¿½s rough heart,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And lower is bowed each head;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And nothing is heard, save a whispered word,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the roar of the shearing-shed.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tall, shy rouser has lost his wits,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his limbs are all astray;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He leaves a fleece on the shearing-board,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And his broom in the shearerן¿½s way.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½s a curse in store for that jackaroo
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As down by the wall he slants,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the ringer bends with his legs askew
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And wishes heן¿½d ן¿½patched them pants.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They are girls from the city. (Our hearts rebel
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As we squint at their dainty feet.)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And they gush and say in a girly way
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That ן¿½the dear little lambsן¿½ are ן¿½sweet.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Bill, the ringer, whoן¿½d scorn the use
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a childish word like ן¿½damn,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would give a pound that his tongue were loose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he tackles a lively lamb.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swift thoughts of homes in the coastal towns,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or rivers and waving grass,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And a weight on our hearts that we cannot define
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That comes as the ladies pass.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the rouser ventures a nervous dig
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the ribs of the next to him;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Barcoo says to his pen-mate: ן¿½Twig
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The style of the last un, Jim.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jim Moonlight gives her a careless glance,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he catches his breath with pain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His strong hand shakes and the sunlights dance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he bends to his work again.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But heן¿½s well disguised in a bristling beard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bronzed skin, and his shearerן¿½s dress;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And whatever Jim Moonlight hoped or feared
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were hard for his mates to guess.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jim Moonlight, wiping his broad, white brow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Explains, with a doleful smile:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A stitch in the side,ן¿½ and ן¿½heן¿½s all right nowן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he leans on the beam awhile,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And gazes out in the blazing noon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the clearing, brown and bare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She has come and gone, like a breath of June,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In Decemberן¿½s heat and glare.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The bushmen are big rough boys at the best,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With hearts of a larger growth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But they hide those hearts with a brutal jest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the pain with a reckless oath.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though the Bills and Jims of the bush-bard sing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of their life loves, lost or dead,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The love of a girl is a sacred thing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not voiced in a shearing-shed.
meaning:
Exploring the themes of classic, Henry Lawson delivers a powerful performance in "When The Ladies Come To The Shearing Shed"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
